sections.name: Executive Summary
sections.description: A 1-page overview of the entire business plan. Grant reviewers often read ONLY this section, so it must be compelling.
sections.grant_tip: Many grant applications ask for a 'project summary' — your Executive Summary can be adapted for this.
common_mistakes: Being too vague — 'We help people' doesn't tell the reviewer anything
Writing it first instead of last — the best executive summaries are written after all other sections
Making it too long — keep it to 1 page maximum

sections.name: Business Description
sections.description: Detailed overview of what the business does, its legal structure, history, and the problem it solves.
sections.grant_tip: Grants love a clear problem-solution narrative. Frame your business as solving a real community need.
common_mistakes: Forgetting to mention legal structure (LLC, sole prop, etc.)
Not connecting the business to a real market need
Being too technical — write for a general audience

sections.name: Market Analysis
sections.description: Research on target customers, market size, and competitive landscape.
sections.grant_tip: Include local demographic data if applying for community development grants. Census.gov is a free resource.
common_mistakes: Saying 'everyone is my customer' — be specific
Ignoring competitors — reviewers know they exist
Not including any numbers — even rough estimates help

sections.name: Organization & Management
sections.description: Team structure, owner qualifications, and advisory support.
sections.grant_tip: Highlight any relevant certifications, degrees, or years of experience. Grants fund PEOPLE, not just ideas.
common_mistakes: Being too humble about your own qualifications
Not mentioning advisors or mentors — they add credibility
Forgetting to include planned hires

sections.name: Products & Services
sections.description: Detailed breakdown of offerings, pricing, costs, and future plans.
sections.grant_tip: If your product or service is innovative, explain what makes it different clearly.
common_mistakes: Not knowing your own costs — track everything
Listing too many offerings — focus on your top 3-5
Not explaining your pricing rationale

sections.name: Marketing & Sales Strategy
sections.description: How the business attracts and retains customers.
sections.grant_tip: Show that you have a plan beyond 'word of mouth.' Even free strategies like social media count.
common_mistakes: No budget at all — even $50/month shows intentionality
Being vague — 'social media' is not a strategy, 'posting 3x/week on Instagram with customer testimonials' is
Not measuring results — mention any metrics you track

sections.name: Financial Projections
sections.grant_tip: Be honest about where you are. Grants for micro-businesses expect modest numbers. Credibility matters more than big projections.
common_mistakes: Wildly optimistic projections with no basis
Not including expenses — revenue means nothing without costs
Being embarrassed about small numbers — $2K/month is a real business

sections.name: Funding Request
sections.description: How much funding is needed, specific use of funds, and expected impact.
sections.grant_tip: Be SPECIFIC. '$10,000 for a commercial oven, display case, and 3 months of rent' beats '$10,000 for business growth.'
common_mistakes: Asking for a round number with no breakdown
Not connecting the funding to measurable outcomes
Forgetting to mention the timeline for spending
